Case study — 2025

ASCAutoPayroll

Twelve identical form fields, every week, in an interface that predates the students using it. The schedule was already known — the typing was the only variable.

Stack
Python · Selenium
Type
Browser automation
Source
github.com/arenvista/ASCAutoPayroll ↗

The problem

Student employees submit hours through PeopleSoft: log in, navigate several frames deep, enter in and out times for each day, save, submit. The shifts are usually the same every week, which makes the whole exercise a transcription job — and one with real consequences, since a missed submission means a missed paycheck.

The approach

Selenium drives a real browser session, because PeopleSoft has no usable API and its interface is built from nested frames and generated element IDs — the kind of page that defeats a plain HTTP client but is perfectly navigable if you're pretending to be a person.

A schedule file describes the recurring shifts. The tool logs in, walks to the timesheet for the current pay period, fills each row from that schedule, and stops before submission so the hours can be checked by an actual human first.

Working against a hostile page

  • Generated IDs change between renders, so elements are located by structure and label rather than by whatever ID the page happened to emit.
  • Every action waits on a condition instead of a timer — PeopleSoft's partial page refreshes are unpredictable, and fixed sleeps are how these tools rot.
  • Credentials come from the environment, never the repository, and the session is torn down when the run ends.
  • A dry run fills the form and stops, so a broken selector shows up as a wrong-looking page rather than a wrong submission.

What I took away

The part I'd defend is the stop. It would have been trivial to click submit at the end of the run, and marginally more convenient — but a tool that files payroll on your behalf is one bad selector away from filing something wrong, silently, on a document you're accountable for. Automating the tedium and leaving the confirmation is the whole design.
